title: Mini's First Vote
description: Mini is in Standard 6, old enough to vote in the school elections. Soon, she has to help choose the school captain. Whom should she vote for?

pages.text:
Mini is a student in Standard 6.
She has one dimple, two bouncy ponytails and a busy,
buzzing mind that runs in three directions at once.
Sometimes, a busy, buzzing mind is useful.
Sometimes it isn’t.

It is useful when Mini is doing homework, cuddling
the cat and dreaming of the summer holidays.
All at the same time.

It is inconvenient when Mini goes to her favourite sweet shop.
One part of her mind wants laddoo. Another wants gulab jamun. A third wants barfi.
“I will choose tomorrow,” Mini tells her mother.
Mini can never, ever decide.

This week, though, Mini has to make a big decision.
Bigger than picking sweets. Bigger than choosing a book from the school library.
This week, Mini has to vote to elect the next school captain.

Arun is in Standard 8. He has already voted twice—last year, and the year before.
“Whom should I vote for?” Mini asks, when they are on the way to school on Monday morning.
“It’s your vote,” Arun says. “You must decide.”
“But how?” Mini asks, looking confused.
“Read the posters,” Arun tells her. “Listen to the speeches.
See if the promises make sense. Then you decide whom to vote for.”

Mini has never voted before. Students can only vote after they reach Standard 6.
“You have to choose the best person for the job,” Arun says.
Four students are running for the position of school captain.
The walls of the school are bright with their posters and promises.
At break, Mini and Mohsin read all the posters.

Diya’s posters are rainbow-coloured and sparkly. After all, she wins every art competition.
Pratik’s posters promise more sports—cricket, football and chess. He is the captain of the cricket team.
Rubina’s posters are plain and a little untidy. They talk about making the school a more fun place.
VOTE
FOR
DIYA
VOTE
FOR
PRATIK
VOTE FOR
RUBINA
For better school experience

Akshay’s posters are full of pictures of Akshay. He is the most fashionable, confident boy in the school. His posters are bigger than the blackboard.
“Such big posters are against the rules,” Mohsin whispers
to Mini. “But Akshay will win. He gets whatever he wants.”
VOTE FOR
AKSHAY

All day, Mini’s mind runs in three directions.
“Should I vote for Diya?” she asks her mother in the evening. “Her posters are pretty.”
“Or for Akshay?” she asks her father at dinner. “His posters cover the entire wall.”

“Or for Pratik?” she wonders at bedtime. “Mohsin likes him best.”
“It’s your vote,” her mother says, switching off the light. “Nobody can tell you.”
Mini wishes that she didn’t have to decide. “The only thing I know is that I don’t really like Rubina’s posters much,” she says.

On Tuesday, the candidates distribute badges.
Diya’s badges are shaped like stars
Pratik’s badges are like circles.

Akshay’s badges are as big as kitchen towels. They have chocolates taped to them.
Rubina’s badges are small and messy. On each badge she has written ways to make the school a more fun place.
Stop
Bullying
Keep Your Classrooms Clean
Music at
Breaktime

Mini eats a chocolate on the way home.
“Maybe I’ll vote for Akshay,” she says. “He will hand out chocolates if he wins.”
“Akshay is breaking all the rules,” Arun says. “He is not supposed to make such big posters, or give chocolates,
or force people to vote for him. But he will win anyway.”

Wednesday is the day for speeches.
Diya’s speech is as pretty as her posters. She talks about more art and dance classes in the school.
Pratik is full of energy. His speech is about sports.
Akshay is loud. His speech is about how great he is.
Rubina is shy. Her speech is packed with ideas—ideas that Mini loves.

Mini and Mohsin are eating chutney sandwiches when Bhaskar marches up to them.
“You better vote for Akshay,” he says, aiming a kick at Mohsin’s ankle.
“Nobody can tell us whom to vote for,” Mini says. “You can’t threaten us.”
“Really?” Bhaskar jeers.

Mohsin is scared. Mini is angry.
“Our vote is secret,” she says. “We don’t need to be afraid.”
“Aren’t you voting for Akshay?” Mohsin asks.
Mini shakes her head. For the first time in days she is
not confused.
She whispers a name in Mohsin’s ear.

On Thursday, Mini stands in line. When it is her turn, she walks up to the ballot box, picks up a square of paper and writes a name in block letters. She folds the paper and puts it into the box.
Whom do you think Mini voted for?
Whom would you vote for?
BALLOT BOX

At lunchtime, Principal Kapoor announces the results.
Mini is sad that Rubina has lost. Rubina’s posters were plain, her badges were ordinary and her speech was soft and nervous. But her ideas were so wonderful that she got Mini’s vote.
Secretly, Mini is happy that Akshay has lost.
Pratik is their new school captain.
CAPTAIN

At home, Mini tells her mother about the elections.
“I think I have learnt to choose,” she says. “And I think I have learnt a little about voting. I can’t wait for the next school elections.”
BALLOT BOX
